title: Scaling Collapse Reveals Universal Dynamics in Compute-Optimally Trained Neural Networks

reviews.summary: This paper reports a striking empirical regularity in training dynamics: when models are trained under compute-optimal horizons, entire loss curves from different model sizes collapse onto a single universal curve after normalizing both compute and reducible loss by their final values. With learning-rate decay, this collapse becomes so tight that cross-scale variability falls below the per-model seed noise, a regime the authors call supercollapse. The paper explains (i) collapse under constant learning rate via a sum-of-power-laws scaling model and compute-optimal allocation, and (ii) collapse and supercollapse under decaying schedules via a simple stochastic-noise model that predicts schedule-dependent loss curves and a quantitative Δ ∝ √η(1 − τ̂) scaling for the collapse deviation.
strengths: Introduces the notion of supercollapse: a regime where normalized cross-scale variation of loss curves falls below the single-seed noise floor, and connects it to control-variate–like variance cancellation via final-loss normalization.
Provides a clean theoretical link between compute-optimal allocation and collapse for sum-of-power-laws loss functions; the “balance of power laws” argument is elegant and compelling.

Proposes a simple, practically usable model of SGD noise under learning-rate schedules that, despite strong approximations, accurately predicts schedule-specific loss curves with a single shared scalar α across models, schedules, and horizons.

Demonstrates collapse and supercollapse across two architectures (transformers and MLPs), two tasks (CIFAR-5M next-token prediction, Lichess chess), multiple widths (roughly 10–80M parameters), and several learning-rate schedules (linear/cosine with and without holdouts).
Quantifies collapse via a clear metric Δ and compares it to a per-model noise floor σ, carefully distinguishing seed-induced variability from cross-scale differences.

Provides falsification-style tests: collapse degrades under suboptimal scaling choices (e.g., no µP parameterization or mis-specified data exponent γ), making the phenomenon a potentially sensitive diagnostic.
The normalization choices, definitions (Δ, σ), and the role of L0 as irreducible loss are well motivated, with visualizations that make the phenomena easy to grasp.
The theory sections are modular and transparent about assumptions (quadratic approximation, slowly varying Hessian/Σ, schedule defined in normalized compute).
Suggests a joint scaling limit (model size and training horizon growing together) that preserves dynamical similarity, complementing infinite-width/depth limits that typically fix time.
If robust, supercollapse could provide a low-cost, high-sensitivity scaling diagnostic to tune exponents and parameterizations without large sweeps or many seeds.
Offers a unifying lens for loss-curve prediction across schedules, potentially informing practical schedule design and hyperparameter transfer.
weaknesses: The stochastic-noise model relies on a quadratic-loss approximation, slowly varying curvature/noise, and a single global α; while surprisingly predictive, this limits theoretical guarantees and may hide schedule- or data-dependent failure modes.

The compute-optimal horizon t*(p) is estimated under constant learning rate and reused for decayed schedules; although the authors cite prior work suggesting near-optimality up to constants, empirical verification that t*(p) remains near-optimal per schedule is limited.

The comparison between Δ (normalized by final stochastic loss) and σ (per-model relative fluctuation of reducible loss) blends distinct normalizations; while the control-variate interpretation is insightful, the “below noise floor” claim would be stronger with a matched normalization baseline.

Scale is modest (≤ ~80M parameters) and on proxy tasks (CIFAR-5M, character-level chess). Claims of universality would benefit from larger, more realistic language modeling setups and co-scaling ladders (width, depth, batch size, weight decay).

Seed counts are sometimes small and heterogeneous across settings; for some plots, it is unclear whether σ and Δ are averaged over sufficient seeds to robustly separate collapse from noise, especially in the decay tail where curves converge.
Sensitivity to the fitted L0 is shown qualitatively; more systematic uncertainty quantification of L0 estimation and its effect on collapse metrics would strengthen the case.
The mapping from discrete steps to gradient-flow time τ and normalized τ̂ could be specified more precisely to facilitate reproduction, including how η(τ) is computed with Adam/µP preconditioning.
The derivation and role of the schedule mapping y(x) (Eq. 21) are somewhat terse; more intuition/examples would help.

While many scaling-law and theory papers are cited, a more explicit empirical comparison to alternative loss-curve predictors (e.g., Tissue et al., Luo et al.) in the same settings would contextualize the benefits/limitations of the proposed model beyond qualitative overlays.

The paper could better relate to dynamical mean-field accounts of joint time–size scaling (e.g., Bordelon et al. 2024 DMFT) by clarifying where predictions concur/diverge, especially regarding late-time finite-size effects.
reviews.questions:
1. How sensitive is supercollapse to the precise estimation of t*(p)? If you recompute schedule-specific compute-optimal horizons, do the normalized curves and Δ-vs-σ comparisons change materially?
2. Could you provide quantitative error metrics (e.g., MAE/RMSE) comparing your loss-curve predictor (Eq. 18 with one α) against Tissue et al. (2024) and Luo et al. (2025) on the same runs? This would clarify the practical predictive advantage.
3. The comparison of Δ to σ mixes different normalizations (final-loss-normalized vs instantaneous). Can you report a matched-baseline σ̃ that also uses control-variate normalization within each seed (e.g., variability of L(t)/L(t*) across seeds at fixed p) to more rigorously substantiate “below noise floor”?
4. How robust is the Tr(Σ)/L ≈ h(x) universality across optimizers (e.g., AdamW vs SGD+momentum) and parameterizations (µP vs standard)? Have you tested other datasets where irreducible loss is a large fraction of total (e.g., language with limited context)?
5. Can you probe collapse under co-scaling ladders (jointly scaling width, depth, batch size, and weight decay) and report whether the same normalization still yields supercollapse? If not, what modifications are needed?
6. When you perturb γ away from optimal, can you quantify how the collapse deviation grows (e.g., as a function of |γ − γ*|) and whether this can be used to estimate γ* with confidence intervals?
7. The α parameter is global across settings. Is α interpretable in terms of batch size, curvature scale, or optimizer statistics? Can it be predicted rather than fitted?
reviews.assessment: This paper identifies and explains a compelling and practically useful regularity in training dynamics: compute-optimal training induces a universal loss-curve shape across model sizes, and learning-rate decay drives a supercollapse where cross-scale differences drop below per-seed noise due to control-variate cancellation. The theoretical components are simple yet insightful (power-law balance; noise model leading to Δ ∝ √η(1 − τ̂)), and the empirical evidence—though at modest scales and on proxy datasets—is careful, quantitative, and includes informative ablations that demonstrate diagnostic value. The main reservations are the limited scale/scope of experiments, reliance on a fitted α and on constant-LR t*(p) across schedules, and the normalization choices in the Δ vs σ comparison. Nonetheless, the work is novel, thought-provoking, and likely to influence both practical scaling diagnostics and future theory on joint scaling limits. I lean positive and recommend acceptance after minor clarifications and, where feasible, small additional analyses to strengthen the statistical and comparative claims.
